title: Rust on White Cotton
stainChemistry: Rust (iron oxide, Fe2O3) is an inorganic metal stain, not an organic dye. On white cotton, iron oxide particles lodge in fiber texture and bond through electrostatic interactions. Standard detergents and bleach are ineffective — bleach actually sets rust stains darker by further oxidizing the iron. Only reducing agents or chelating agents can dissolve iron oxide back into soluble form.
whyThisWorks: A reducing agent (sodium hydrosulfite professionally, oxalic acid at consumer level) converts insoluble iron oxide back into soluble iron ions on white cotton, which then rinse away. Lemon juice or white vinegar provide mild acid chelation for light rust. The key rule: NEVER use chlorine bleach on rust — it oxidizes the iron further, making the stain permanent.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) to the rust stain — this converts insoluble iron oxide back to soluble form
2. Flush with NSD and cool water to remove dissolved iron
3. If rust persists: repeat sodium hydrosulfite application
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use chlorine bleach on rust — it oxidizes iron further, making the stain permanent

steps: 1. Apply white vinegar or lemon juice to the rust stain. Let sit
2. Sprinkle salt over the vinegar-soaked area for mild abrasion
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat application if needed
5. Air dry — do NOT use heat
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional — they have specialized dye-stripping agents
warnings: Never rub dye stains — rubbing spreads the color

neverDo: Avoid prolonged exposure to concentrated acid
fiberSensitivities: Most chemically resistant textile — tolerates aggressive treatment
Can handle chlorine bleach, hydrogen peroxide, oxygen bleach

escalation.when: If the dye has penetrated the fiber (no improvement after 3 solvent passes), or if the fabric is expensive/delicate, or if dye continues to spread during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is rust on white cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the color still actively spreading or has it dried?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first to ensure fabric tolerates oxalic acid solution. On white cotton, oxalic acid is safer than on colored fabrics.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Oxalic acid is a strong chemical — handle with care and ensure good ventilation.

homeSolutions.instruction: Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface.
Rust stains respond specifically to oxalic acid. Apply oxalic acid (crystals dissolved in minimal cool water) to a white cloth and dab the rust stain gently from center outward.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Oxalic acid can be corrosive — avoid direct skin contact and ensure proper ventilation.

homeSolutions.instruction: Allow the oxalic acid to work on the stain for 2-3 minutes, re-dabbing as needed with fresh cloth.
Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working from center outward, ensuring all oxalic acid residue is removed.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Complete removal of oxalic acid is essential to prevent fiber damage.

homeSolutions.instruction: For white cotton, oxalic acid treatment is typically effective. If rust stain persists after treatment, consult a professional cleaner.
